excerpt: A concise, AI native and production-grade starter template for full-stack applications using pnpm, Turborepo and Cloudflare Workers for edge-native execution 🚚⛅ - louisbrulenaudet/monorepo-template
content_html:
Monorepo starter based on pnpm with Cloudflare, Hono, React, Vite and Tailwind 🚚⛅

A minimal, production-oriented monorepo starter built on pnpm workspaces with Turborepo, Cloudflare Workers, Hono, React (Vite), Tailwind CSS v4, and TanStack Router/Query. AI-ready, designed for edge deployment, and structured for production projects that scale.

Architecture Overview

Monorepo Structure

Starter apps today (worker-api, front-app). Prefixes below describe how the repo grows.

monorepo/
├── apps/                    # Workers and frontends
│   ├── worker-api/          # REST API gateway
│   └── front-app/           # React SPA (Vite + TanStack)
├── packages/                # Shared @repo/* packages
│   ├── dtos-common/         # Zod wire contracts (api / rpc / queue / webhook)
│   ├── enums-common/        # Shared constrained string values (`as const`)
│   └── typescript-config/   # TypeScript configuration presets
├── make/                    # Shared Makefile fragments (see make/README.md)
├── hooks/                   # AI agent hooks (not Husky - see hooks/README.md)
├── package.json             # Root package configuration
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml      # Workspace configuration
├── turbo.json               # Turborepo configuration
└── tsconfig.json            # Root TypeScript configuration

Architecture Components

The monorepo is organized into two main categories: Backend Services and Frontend Applications, plus Shared Packages for common functionality.

flowchart TB
  subgraph entry [Public entry]
    direction LR
    Front["front-* :517x"]
    Ext["External providers"]
    McpClients["MCP clients"]
  end

  subgraph publicWorkers [Public Workers]
    direction LR
    Gateway["worker-api :8700"]
    Webhook["webhook-* :876x"]
    Mcp["mcp-* :878x"]
  end

  subgraph privateWorkers [Private Workers]
    direction LR
    Biz["worker-* (RPC only)"]
    Queue["queue-*"]
  end

  subgraph shared [Shared packages]
    direction LR
    Enums["@repo/enums-common"]
    DTOs["@repo/dtos-common"]
    Enums --> DTOs
  end

  Front --> Gateway
  Ext --> Webhook
  McpClients --> Mcp

  Gateway --> Biz
  Webhook --> Biz
  Mcp --> Biz

  Gateway --> Queue
  Webhook --> Queue
  Biz --> Queue

  Front -.-> shared
  publicWorkers -.-> shared
  privateWorkers -.-> shared
Loading

Backend Services

Cloudflare Workers are organized by runtime role:

  • worker-api - Public HTTP gateway (Hono): CORS, validation, routing; coordinates internal Workers via RPC.
  • worker-* - Business logic over service-binding RPC only (no public routes in production). May own Drizzle schema under src/db/ and that database’s binding (exclusive owner).
  • queue-* - Queue-only consumers (queue() handler). Messages can be produced by worker-api, worker-*, or webhook-*. Use dual-handler layout when a local HTTP debug path is useful.
  • webhook-* - Public HTTP ingress for external provider callbacks; forward work via RPC or queues.
  • mcp-* - Public HTTP MCP servers; thin tools that call worker-* over RPC.

Do not create shared packages/db-* schema packages. Put Drizzle schema under the owning app’s src/db/ and keep one DB binding owner. Other apps reach that data via service-binding RPC (or a queue) - do not attach the same DB binding to multiple apps.

Frontend Applications

  • front-app - React SPA (Vite 8, Tailwind v4, TanStack Router/Query) deployed on Cloudflare Workers. Talks to worker-api over HTTP only - never via service bindings.

Where to put things

Task Location
New API route apps/worker-api/src/routes/<feature>.ts → mount in src/index.ts
HTTP Zod schemas packages/dtos-common/src/api/<feature>.ts
RPC / queue / webhook schemas packages/dtos-common/src/<layer>/<feature>.ts (layer: rpc, queue, or webhook)
Shared string value set packages/enums-common/src/
Worker-local value set apps/<worker>/src/enums/
DB schema (one owner) apps/<owner>/src/db/ - never packages/db-*
Frontend API client apps/front-app/src/services/worker-api/<feature>.ts
Frontend page + route apps/front-app/src/pages/ + src/routes/
Local Worker secrets apps/<worker>/.dev.vars (from .dev.vars.example)
Frontend env apps/front-app/.env.local (from .env.example)

Getting Started

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 22+ (see root package.json engines); we recommend fnm for version management
  • pnpm via the root packageManager field (Corepack recommended)
  • Cloudflare account only if you need make login / deploy / remote Worker features

Install and prepare

make install
make login     # optional - Cloudflare auth for remote Wrangler features
make prepare   # Husky pre-commit hooks

Copy env templates before the first run:

  • Workers: apps/<worker>/.dev.vars.example → .dev.vars
  • Frontend: apps/front-app/.env.example → .env.local

Notes:

  • Prefer make install over raw pnpm install so workspace links stay consistent.
  • This repo pins pnpm via packageManager in the root package.json.

First successful run (verify locally)

  1. Start all dev servers from the repo root: 
    make dev
  2. Verify the API: GET http://localhost:8700/api/v1/health
  3. Open the frontend: http://localhost:5174

Focused work on one package: make dev SCOPE=worker-api (see Scoping).

Make Commands

Command Description
install Install and link workspace packages
install-frozen Install with frozen lockfile (CI)
login Login to Cloudflare (repo-pinned Wrangler)
update Update dependencies to latest (rewrites pnpm catalog)
check Lint + format check (no typecheck)
ci Lint + format + check-types (local PR gate)
deploy Deploy all apps/workers (via Turborepo)
build Build all packages and apps (via Turborepo)
format Auto-fix formatting with oxfmt
lint Auto-fix lint issues with oxlint
dev Start all dev servers (via Turborepo)
preview Preview production builds locally (via Turborepo)
types Generate worker-configuration.d.ts in apps
check-types TypeScript across all workers and packages
prepare Install or reinstall Husky git hooks
husky-status Show Husky hooks status
skills-update Refresh locked agent skills (see make/README.md)

Scoping (pnpm / Turborepo)

Optional variables on any turbo-backed root target (dev, build, ci, …):

Variable Effect Example
SCOPE --filter=<package> make dev SCOPE=worker-api
FILTER Raw turbo filter expression make build FILTER=...front-app...
AFFECTED Only changed packages vs base make ci AFFECTED=1

Development ports

Mnemonic: 87xx = Workers (gateway → business → queue → webhook → MCP → reserve). Frontends use Vite’s 51xx / 41xx.

Role Prefix Local HTTP ports
HTTP gateway worker-api 8700–8709
Business worker (RPC) worker-* 8710–8739
Queue-only consumer queue-* 8740–8759
Webhook ingress webhook-* 8760–8779
MCP server mcp-* 8780–8789
Growth reserve - 8790–8799
Frontend (Vite) front-* 5170–5199 (dev), 4170–4199 (preview)

Assigned registry

Service Path Dev Preview
worker-api apps/worker-api/wrangler.jsonc 8700 -
front-app apps/front-app/vite.config.ts 5174 4174

Notes:

  • Workers: set dev.port in wrangler.jsonc and monorepo.devPort in package.json. Use inspector_port: 0.
  • Frontends: set Vite server.port / preview.port with strictPort: true.
  • Assign the next free port in the role’s range. RPC and queue-only apps still get a local port for standalone wrangler dev, but have no public URL in production.
  • Prefer multi-config local runs when testing bindings (first -c is HTTP-primary).

1. Create a New Cloudflare Worker

App Naming Nomenclature

Purpose Prefix Example
HTTP gateway worker-api (sticky) worker-api
Business logic (RPC) worker- worker-account
Queue-only consumer queue- queue-email
Webhook ingress webhook- webhook-example
MCP server mcp- mcp-tools
Frontend application front- front-app

Key Distinctions

  • Gateway (worker-api): Public HTTP only; validates requests and calls worker-* over RPC.
  • Business Workers (worker-*): RPC-only in production (WorkerEntrypoint); may own Drizzle schema under src/db/ and that database’s binding (exclusive). If they also consume queues, keep this prefix and use the dual-handler layout.
  • Queue-only (queue-*): queue() consumers with no public HTTP in production; may own schema when they are the sole writer for that data.
  • Webhook Workers (webhook-*): Public HTTP for external callbacks; forward via RPC or queues.
  • MCP Servers (mcp-*): Public HTTP MCP transport; thin tools that call worker-* over RPC - never rotate long-lived credentials on this surface.
  • Frontends (front-*): React + Vite; HTTP to the gateway only - never service bindings.
  • Do not create shared packages/db-* schema packages.

Scaffold checklist (copy from an existing app)

There is no generator CLI. Copy the closest sibling under apps/ and wire it into the monorepo:

  1. Copy apps/worker-api (or another closest match) to apps/<prefix-name> (e.g. apps/worker-account).
  2. Rename package.json name, wrangler.jsonc name, and any display strings.
  3. Assign ports from the Development ports registry - set dev.port / inspector_port: 0 in wrangler.jsonc and monorepo.devPort in package.json.
  4. Keep the 4-line Makefile that includes make/app.mk (see make/README.md).
  5. Extend @repo/typescript-config/workers.json (or the matching preset); add .dev.vars.example.
  6. Install and typegen: 
    make install
make types

Copy wrangler patterns (compatibility_flags, observability, env.staging / env.production) from the existing app - see .cursor/rules/backend/workers-config.mdc.

2. Develop a Specific Worker

Prefer scoped make from the repo root:

make dev SCOPE=worker-name

Or use the worker's Makefile:

cd apps/worker-name
make dev
  • This runs the dev script defined in apps/worker-name/package.json
  • Open the port shown in your terminal (for example, http://localhost:8721)
  • Each worker Makefile exposes make dev, make format, make lint, make types, make check-types, make deploy

Testing Service Bindings Between Workers

Prefer a single multi-config wrangler dev (first -c is HTTP-primary):

wrangler dev -c apps/worker-api/wrangler.jsonc -c apps/worker-account/wrangler.jsonc

Or run each Worker in its own terminal (cd apps/worker-account && make dev, then cd apps/worker-api && make dev) and confirm service bindings show as connected in the wrangler output.

Dual-Handler Pattern

Use this layout for queue-* apps and for worker-* apps that also consume queues:

src/
├── handlers/
│   ├── request.ts    # Optional HTTP (local debug only)
│   └── message.ts    # Queue message consumption
├── services/         # Shared business logic
└── index.ts         # Minimal delegation entry point
  • queue-*: queue-only in production (no public HTTP).
  • worker-* with queues: keep the business prefix; expose RPC and optionally dual-handler HTTP for local testing.

3. Environment Configuration

Each worker uses environment-specific configuration. Frontends use Vite env files (see apps/front-app/README.md).

Development Environment

  • Workers: .dev.vars (from .dev.vars.example) for local secrets and overrides
  • Frontend: .env.local (from .env.example) - only VITE_* keys reach the browser

Staging/Production Environments

  • Configuration: env.staging and env.production blocks in wrangler.jsonc
  • Deploy: wrangler deploy --env staging or --env production
  • Service Bindings: Connected to deployed workers

Environment Variables Example

Multi-worker local dev

When service bindings connect Workers, run each in a separate terminal, or use multiple -c flags (first config is HTTP-primary):

wrangler dev -c apps/worker-api/wrangler.jsonc -c apps/worker-example/wrangler.jsonc

Service Binding Configuration

4. Deploy Your Workers

  • Deploy all workers:

    make deploy

  • Deploy a specific worker:

    make deploy SCOPE=worker-name
# or: cd apps/worker-name && make deploy

Best Practices

Architecture Best Practices

  • Colocate schema under the owning Worker’s src/db/: never a shared packages/db-* package; never share the same DB binding across apps - other apps use RPC (or a queue)
  • Implement dual-handler pattern: For queue consumers, separate message handling from optional local HTTP debug handlers
  • Use service bindings (RPC): For inter-worker communication instead of HTTP calls
  • Maintain clear separation of concerns: Each Worker has a specific runtime role (gateway, business, queue, webhook, MCP, frontend)

Development Best Practices

  • Always run make install after adding workers or dependencies
  • Use make dev SCOPE=<package> for focused development on one app (plain make dev starts everything)
  • Follow naming conventions: worker-*, queue-*, webhook-*, mcp-*, front-*
  • Use appropriate port ranges: see Development ports
  • Test service bindings: Verify connections between workers before deployment

Code Quality Best Practices

  • Use strict TypeScript everywhere: Enforce type safety across all workers
  • Validate all data with Zod schemas: Shared contracts live in @repo/dtos-common
  • Return explicit HTTP status codes and typed JSON errors at the gateway boundary
  • Follow OXC formatting standards: Consistent code style across the monorepo (oxfmt + oxlint)
  • Use shared packages: Leverage @repo/* packages for wire contracts and configs
  • Run make ci before opening a PR (see Contribution)

Service Communication

Workers communicate via service bindings:

// Call a business Worker over RPC
const result = await env.ACCOUNT_SERVICE.getAccount(userId);

// Call another business Worker
const completion = await env.WORKER_GENAI.completion(request);

Git Hooks

This repo has two hook systems. They do not replace each other:

System When it runs Docs
Husky (.husky/) Human git commit This section
Agent hooks (hooks/) Cursor / Claude Code tool loop hooks/README.md 
flowchart LR
  subgraph human [Human git]
    Commit["git commit"] --> Husky[".husky/pre-commit"]
    Husky --> Oxc["oxfmt + oxlint"]
  end
  subgraph agent [AI agent loop]
    Shell["beforeShellExecution"] --> GitHooks["hooks/git/*"]
    Edit["afterFileEdit"] --> Quality["hooks/quality/*"]
  end
Loading

Husky pre-commit

Husky formats staged files with oxfmt (git-format-staged) and runs the repository-wide oxlint safe fixer before each commit.

make prepare       # install / reinstall hooks
make husky-status  # verify hooks are executable

Contribution

  • Run make ci before opening a PR (lint + format + check-types). GitHub CI also runs an affected build.
  • Wire-format changes: update @repo/dtos-common and every producer/consumer in the same PR (HTTP → worker-api + front-app).
  • When you add endpoints, bindings, or env vars, update the relevant app/package README and AGENTS.md.

AI agent instructions

  • AGENTS.md - cross-tool project conventions and Cursor's root instructions.
  • CLAUDE.md - Claude Code entry point; imports AGENTS.md per Claude memory docs.
  • Per-app/package - each workspace has matching AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md.
  • hooks/README.md - shared agent hook scripts (not Husky).
  • Rules - mirrored trees under .cursor/rules/**/*.mdc and .claude/rules/**/*.md.
  • Skills - source of truth under .agents/skills/ (see skill monorepo-agent-setup).
  • Security - .cursorignore reduces model context but is not an access-control boundary.

Shared Packages (@repo/*)

Local packages under packages/. Each package has its own README.

Available Shared Packages

  • @repo/dtos-common - Zod wire contracts via subpaths: /api, /rpc, /queue, /webhook
  • @repo/enums-common - Shared constrained string values (as const objects)
  • @repo/typescript-config - TypeScript presets (strict.json, workers.json, workers-lib.json, vite-react.json, vite-node.json)

Benefits of Shared Packages

  • Code sharing: Eliminate duplication across workers
  • Consistency: Centralized configurations and utilities
  • Easy updates: Update once, propagate to all workers
  • Type safety: Shared TypeScript configurations ensure consistency

How to Use Shared Packages

  1. Add to your worker's package.json:

    "dependencies": {
  "@repo/dtos-common": "workspace:*",
  "@repo/enums-common": "workspace:*",
  "@repo/typescript-config": "workspace:*"
}

  2. Import and use in your code:

    import { HttpMethod } from "@repo/enums-common";
import { HealthResponseSchema } from "@repo/dtos-common/api";

  3. Development workflow:

    • Changes in shared packages are reflected immediately in workers
    • Run make install after adding new shared package dependencies

More Information

  • pnpm workspace protocol docs
  • Turborepo monorepo docs

Service Bindings

Service bindings allow Workers to communicate directly with each other without going through publicly accessible URLs. They provide the separation of concerns that microservice architectures offer, without configuration pain, performance overhead, or the need to learn RPC protocols.

Key Benefits

  • Zero overhead: Workers run on the same thread, providing zero latency
  • Not just HTTP: Direct method calls between Workers using JavaScript functions
  • No additional costs: Service bindings don't increase Cloudflare pricing
  • Secure communication: No public URLs required

Configuration

Add service bindings to your worker's wrangler.jsonc:

RPC Method Invocation

RPC requires the callee to extend WorkerEntrypoint and expose public methods. The caller gets typed env.BINDING.method() stubs from wrangler types when you pass every bound Worker's config (see Workers RPC - TypeScript).

Callee (worker-name):

import { WorkerEntrypoint } from "cloudflare:workers";

export default class extends WorkerEntrypoint {
  async fetch() {
    return new Response("ok");
  }
  doSomething(input: string) {
    return { input, ok: true };
  }
}

Caller (e.g. worker-api):

export default {
  async fetch(_request: Request, env: Env): Promise<Response> {
    const result = await env.BUSINESS_LOGIC_SERVICE.doSomething("payload");
    return Response.json(result);
  },
} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;

Regenerate types on the caller after adding bindings:

wrangler types -c ./wrangler.jsonc -c ../worker-name/wrangler.jsonc

Development Environment Workers: .dev.vars (from .dev.vars.example) for local secrets and overrides Frontend: .env.local (from .env.example) - only VITE_* keys reach the browser Staging/Production Environments Configuration: env.staging and env.production blocks in wrangler.jsonc Deploy: wrangler deploy --env staging or --env production Service Bindings: Connected to deployed workers Environment Variables Example Multi-worker local dev When service bindings connect Workers, run each in a separate terminal, or use multiple -c flags (first config is HTTP-primary): wrangler dev -c apps/worker-api/wrangler.jsonc -c apps/worker-example/wrangler.jsonc Service Binding Configuration 4. Deploy Your Workers Deploy all workers: make deploy Deploy a specific worker: make deploy SCOPE=worker-name # or: cd apps/worker-name && make deploy Best Practices Architecture Best Practices Colocate schema under the owning Worker’s src/db/: never a shared packages/db-* package; never share the same DB binding across apps - other apps use RPC (or a queue) Implement dual-handler pattern: For queue consumers, separate message handling from optional local HTTP debug handlers Use service bindings (RPC): For inter-worker communication instead of HTTP calls Maintain clear separation of concerns: Each Worker has a specific runtime role (gateway, business, queue, webhook, MCP, frontend) Development Best Practices Always run make install after adding workers or dependencies Use make dev SCOPE=<package> for focused development on one app (plain make dev starts everything) Follow naming conventions: worker-*, queue-*, webhook-*, mcp-*, front-* Use appropriate port ranges: see Development ports Test service bindings: Verify connections between workers before deployment Code Quality Best Practices Use strict TypeScript everywhere: Enforce type safety across all workers Validate all data with Zod schemas: Shared contracts live in @repo/dtos-common Return explicit HTTP status codes and typed JSON errors at the gateway boundary Follow OXC formatting standards: Consistent code style across the monorepo (oxfmt + oxlint) Use shared packages: Leverage @repo/* packages for wire contracts and configs Run make ci before opening a PR (see Contribution) Service Communication Workers communicate via service bindings: // Call a business Worker over RPC const result = await env.ACCOUNT_SERVICE.getAccount(userId); // Call another business Worker const completion = await env.WORKER_GENAI.completion(request); Git Hooks This repo has two hook systems. They do not replace each other: System When it runs Docs Husky (.husky/) Human git commit This section Agent hooks (hooks/) Cursor / Claude Code tool loop hooks/README.md flowchart LR subgraph human [Human git] Commit["git commit"] --> Husky[".husky/pre-commit"] Husky --> Oxc["oxfmt + oxlint"] end subgraph agent [AI agent loop] Shell["beforeShellExecution"] --> GitHooks["hooks/git/*"] Edit["afterFileEdit"] --> Quality["hooks/quality/*"] end Loading Husky pre-commit Husky formats staged files with oxfmt (git-format-staged) and runs the repository-wide oxlint safe fixer before each commit. make prepare # install / reinstall hooks make husky-status # verify hooks are executable Contribution Run make ci before opening a PR (lint + format + check-types). GitHub CI also runs an affected build. Wire-format changes: update @repo/dtos-common and every producer/consumer in the same PR (HTTP → worker-api + front-app). When you add endpoints, bindings, or env vars, update the relevant app/package README and AGENTS.md. AI agent instructions AGENTS.md - cross-tool project conventions and Cursor's root instructions. CLAUDE.md - Claude Code entry point; imports AGENTS.md per Claude memory docs. Per-app/package - each workspace has matching AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md. hooks/README.md - shared agent hook scripts (not Husky). Rules - mirrored trees under .cursor/rules/**/*.mdc and .claude/rules/**/*.md. Skills - source of truth under .agents/skills/ (see skill monorepo-agent-setup). Security - .cursorignore reduces model context but is not an access-control boundary. Shared Packages (@repo/*) Local packages under packages/. Each package has its own README. Available Shared Packages @repo/dtos-common - Zod wire contracts via subpaths: /api, /rpc, /queue, /webhook @repo/enums-common - Shared constrained string values (as const objects) @repo/typescript-config - TypeScript presets (strict.json, workers.json, workers-lib.json, vite-react.json, vite-node.json) Benefits of Shared Packages Code sharing: Eliminate duplication across workers Consistency: Centralized configurations and utilities Easy updates: Update once, propagate to all workers Type safety: Shared TypeScript configurations ensure consistency How to Use Shared Packages Add to your worker's package.json: "dependencies": { "@repo/dtos-common": "workspace:*", "@repo/enums-common": "workspace:*", "@repo/typescript-config": "workspace:*" } Import and use in your code: import { HttpMethod } from "@repo/enums-common"; import { HealthResponseSchema } from "@repo/dtos-common/api"; Development workflow: Changes in shared packages are reflected immediately in workers Run make install after adding new shared package dependencies More Information pnpm workspace protocol docs Turborepo monorepo docs Service Bindings Service bindings allow Workers to communicate directly with each other without going through publicly accessible URLs. They provide the separation of concerns that microservice architectures offer, without configuration pain, performance overhead, or the need to learn RPC protocols. Key Benefits Zero overhead: Workers run on the same thread, providing zero latency Not just HTTP: Direct method calls between Workers using JavaScript functions No additional costs: Service bindings don't increase Cloudflare pricing Secure communication: No public URLs required Configuration Add service bindings to your worker's wrangler.jsonc: RPC Method Invocation RPC requires the callee to extend WorkerEntrypoint and expose public methods. The caller gets typed env.BINDING.method() stubs from wrangler types when you pass every bound Worker's config (see Workers RPC - TypeScript). Callee (worker-name): import { WorkerEntrypoint } from "cloudflare:workers"; export default class extends WorkerEntrypoint { async fetch() { return new Response("ok"); } doSomething(input: string) { return { input, ok: true }; } } Caller (e.g. worker-api): export default { async fetch(_request: Request, env: Env): Promise<Response> { const result = await env.BUSINESS_LOGIC_SERVICE.doSomething("payload"); return Response.json(result); }, } satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>; Regenerate types on the caller after adding bindings: wrangler types -c ./wrangler.jsonc -c ../worker-name/wrangler.jsonc

content_html_zh:
基于 pnpm 的 Monorepo 启动器，包含 Cloudflare、Hono、React、Vite 和 Tailwind 🚚⛅

一个最小的、面向生产的 monorepo 启动器，基于 pnpm 工作区，包含 Turborepo、Cloudflare Workers、Hono、React (Vite)、Tailwind CSS v4 和 TanStack Router/Query。 人工智能就绪，专为边缘部署而设计，并为可扩展的生产项目而构建。

架构概述

单一回购结构

今天的入门应用程序（worker-api、front-app）。 下面的前缀描述了存储库的增长方式。

monorepo/
├── apps/                    # Workers and frontends
│   ├── worker-api/          # REST API gateway
│   └── front-app/           # React SPA (Vite + TanStack)
├── packages/                # Shared @repo/* packages
│   ├── dtos-common/         # Zod wire contracts (api / rpc / queue / webhook)
│   ├── enums-common/        # Shared constrained string values (`as const`)
│   └── typescript-config/   # TypeScript configuration presets
├── make/                    # Shared Makefile fragments (see make/README.md)
├── hooks/                   # AI agent hooks (not Husky - see hooks/README.md)
├── package.json             # Root package configuration
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml      # Workspace configuration
├── turbo.json               # Turborepo configuration
└── tsconfig.json            # Root TypeScript configuration

架构组件

monorepo 分为两个主要类别：后端服务和前端应用程序，以及用于通用功能的共享包。

flowchart TB
  subgraph entry [Public entry]
    direction LR
    Front["front-* :517x"]
    Ext["External providers"]
    McpClients["MCP clients"]
  end

  subgraph publicWorkers [Public Workers]
    direction LR
    Gateway["worker-api :8700"]
    Webhook["webhook-* :876x"]
    Mcp["mcp-* :878x"]
  end

  subgraph privateWorkers [Private Workers]
    direction LR
    Biz["worker-* (RPC only)"]
    Queue["queue-*"]
  end

  subgraph shared [Shared packages]
    direction LR
    Enums["@repo/enums-common"]
    DTOs["@repo/dtos-common"]
    Enums --> DTOs
  end

  Front --> Gateway
  Ext --> Webhook
  McpClients --> Mcp

  Gateway --> Biz
  Webhook --> Biz
  Mcp --> Biz

  Gateway --> Queue
  Webhook --> Queue
  Biz --> Queue

  Front -.-> shared
  publicWorkers -.-> shared
  privateWorkers -.-> shared
Loading

后端服务

Cloudflare Workers 按运行时角色组织：

  • worker-api - 公共 HTTP 网关 (Hono)：CORS、验证、路由；通过 RPC 协调内部 Worker。
  • worker-* - 仅通过服务绑定 RPC 的业务逻辑（生产中没有公共路由）。 可能拥有 src/db/ 下的 Drizzle 模式以及该数据库的绑定（独占所有者）。
  • queue-* - 仅队列消费者（queue() 处理程序）。 消息可以由worker-api、worker-*或webhook-*生成。 当本地 HTTP 调试路径有用时，请使用双处理程序布局。
  • webhook-* - 用于外部提供者回调的公共 HTTP 入口；通过 RPC 或队列转发工作。
  • mcp-* - 公共 HTTP MCP 服务器；通过 RPC 调用 worker-* 的瘦工具。

不要创建共享包/db-* 架构包。 将 Drizzle 架构放在所属应用程序的 src/db/ 下，并保留一个数据库绑定所有者。 其他应用程序通过服务绑定 RPC（或队列）获取该数据 - 不要将相同的数据库绑定附加到多个应用程序。

前端应用程序

  • front-app -React SPA（Vite 8、Tailwind v4、TanStack 路由器/查询）部署在 Cloudflare Workers 上。 仅通过 HTTP 与worker-api 对话——从不通过服务绑定。

东西放在哪里

Task 地点
新的API路线 apps/worker-api/src/routes/<feature>.ts → 挂载到 src/index.ts
HTTP Zod 架构 包/dtos-common/src/api/<feature>.ts
RPC / 队列 / Webhook 架构 packages/dtos-common/src/<layer>/<feature>.ts（层：rpc、队列或 webhook）
共享字符串值集 包/enums-common/src/
工人本地值集 应用程序/<worker>/src/enums/
数据库模式（一个所有者） apps/<owner>/src/db/ - 从不包/db-*
前端API客户端 应用程序/front-app/src/services/worker-api/<feature>.ts
前端页面+路由 应用程序/front-app/src/pages/ + src/routes/
本地工人的秘密 apps/<worker>/.dev.vars（来自 .dev.vars.example）
前端环境 apps/front-app/.env.local（来自 .env.example）

入门

先决条件

  • Node.js 22+（请参阅根 package.json 引擎）；我们推荐 fnm 进行版本管理
  • pnpm 通过根 packageManager 字段（推荐 Corepack）
  • 仅当您需要登录/部署/远程 Worker 功能时才使用 Cloudflare 帐户

安装和准备

make install
make login     # optional - Cloudflare auth for remote Wrangler features
make prepare   # Husky pre-commit hooks

在第一次运行之前复制环境模板：

  • 工作人员：apps/<worker>/.dev.vars.example → .dev.vars
  • 前端：apps/front-app/.env.example → .env.local

Notes:

  • 优先使用 make install 而不是原始 pnpm install，以便工作区链接保持一致。
  • 此存储库通过根 package.json 中的 packageManager 固定 pnpm。

首次成功运行（本地验证）

  1. 从存储库根目录启动所有开发服务器：make dev
  2. 验证 API：GET http://localhost:8700/api/v1/health
  3. 打开前端：http://localhost:5174

将工作重点放在一个包上：make dev SCOPE=worker-api（请参阅范围界定）。

范围界定（pnpm / Turborepo）

任何 Turbo 支持的根目标上的可选变量（dev、build、ci...）：

开发端口

助记符：87xx = Workers（网关→业务→队列→webhook→MCP→预留）。 前端使用 Vite 的 51xx / 41xx。

仅队列消费者 queue-* 8740–8759

成长储备 - 8790–8799

服务 Path Dev 预览

前端应用程序 应用程序/front-app/vite.config.ts 5174 4174

Notes:

  • Workers：在wrangler.jsonc中设置dev.port，在package.json中设置monorepo.devPort。 使用检查器端口：0。
  • 前端：设置 Vite server.port / Preview.port with strictPort: true。
  • 分配角色范围内的下一个空闲端口。 RPC 和仅队列应用程序仍然获得独立 Wrangler 开发的本地端口，但在生产中没有公共 URL。
  • 测试绑定时首选多配置本地运行（第一个 -c 是 HTTP-primary）。

目的 前缀 例子

主要区别

  • 网关 (worker-api)：仅限公共 HTTP；验证请求并通过 RPC 调用worker-*。
  • Business Workers (worker-*)：生产中仅使用 RPC (WorkerEntrypoint)；可能拥有 src/db/ 下的 Drizzle 模式以及该数据库的绑定（独占）。 如果它们也消耗队列，请保留此前缀并使用双处理程序布局。
  • 仅队列（queue-*）：生产中没有公共 HTTP 的队列（）消费者；当他们是该数据的唯一编写者时，他们可能拥有模式。
  • Webhook Workers (webhook-*)：用于外部回调的公共 HTTP；通过 RPC 或队列转发。
  • MCP 服务器 (mcp-*)：公共 HTTP MCP 传输；通过 RPC 调用 worker-* 的瘦工具 - 永远不会在此表面上轮换长期有效的凭据。
  • 前端（front-*）：React + Vite；仅到网关的 HTTP - 从不服务绑定。
  • 不要创建共享包/db-* 架构包。

脚手架清单（从现有应用程序复制）

没有生成器 CLI。 复制 apps/ 下最接近的同级并将其连接到 monorepo：

  1. 将 apps/worker-api （或其他最接近的匹配）复制到 apps/<prefix-name> （例如 apps/worker-account）。
  2. 重命名 package.json 名称、wrangler.jsonc 名称和任何显示字符串。
  3. 从开发端口注册表分配端口 - 在 wrangler.jsonc 中设置 dev.port /spector_port: 0，在 package.json 中设置 monorepo.devPort。
  4. 保留包含 make/app.mk 的 4 行 Makefile（请参阅 make/README.md）。
  5. 扩展@repo/typescript-config/workers.json（或匹配的预设）；添加.dev.vars.example。
  6. 安装和 typegen：make install make types

从现有应用程序复制牧马人模式（compatibility_flags、observability、env.staging / env.Production） - 请参阅 .cursor/rules/backend/workers-config.mdc。

2. 培养特定工人

更喜欢从 repo 根目录进行作用域 make：

make dev SCOPE=worker-name

或者使用worker的Makefile：

cd apps/worker-name
make dev
  • 这将运行 apps/worker-name/package.json 中定义的开发脚本
  • 打开终端中显示的端口（例如，http://localhost:8721）
  • 每个worker Makefile都公开make dev、make format、make lint、make types、make check-types、make deploy

测试工作人员之间的服务绑定

更喜欢单个多配置牧马人开发（第一个 -c 是 HTTP-primary）：

wrangler dev -c apps/worker-api/wrangler.jsonc -c apps/worker-account/wrangler.jsonc

或者在其自己的终端中运行每个 Worker（cd apps/worker-account && make dev，然后 cd apps/worker-api && make dev）并确认服务绑定在 Wrangler 输出中显示为已连接。

双处理程序模式

将此布局用于队列 * 应用程序和也使用队列的工作程序 * 应用程序：

src/
├── handlers/
│   ├── request.ts    # Optional HTTP (local debug only)
│   └── message.ts    # Queue message consumption
├── services/         # Shared business logic
└── index.ts         # Minimal delegation entry point
  • queue-*：仅在生产中使用队列（无公共 HTTP）。
  • 带有队列的worker-*：保留业务前缀；公开 RPC 和可选的双处理程序 HTTP 以进行本地测试。

最佳实践

架构最佳实践

  • 将 schema 放在所属 Worker 的 src/db/ 下：绝不是共享的 packages/db-* 包；切勿在应用程序之间共享相同的数据库绑定 - 其他应用程序使用 RPC（或队列）
  • 实现双处理程序模式：对于队列使用者，将消息处理与可选的本地 HTTP 调试处理程序分开
  • 使用服务绑定 (RPC)：用于工作人员间通信而不是 HTTP 调用
  • 保持清晰的关注点分离：每个 Worker 都有特定的运行时角色（网关、业务、队列、webhook、MCP、前端）

开发最佳实践

  • 添加工作程序或依赖项后始终运行 make install
  • 使用 make dev SCOPE=<package> 集中开发一个应用程序（简单的 make dev 启动所有内容）
  • 遵循命名约定：worker-*、queue-*、webhook-*、mcp-*、front-*
  • 使用适当的端口范围：请参阅开发端口
  • 测试服务绑定：在部署之前验证工作人员之间的连接

代码质量最佳实践

  • 到处使用严格的 TypeScript：在所有工作人员中强制执行类型安全
  • 使用 Zod 模式验证所有数据：共享合约位于 @repo/dtos-common
  • 在网关边界返回显式 HTTP 状态代码和键入的 JSON 错误
  • 遵循 OXC 格式化标准：整个 monorepo 保持一致的代码风格 (oxfmt + oxlint)
  • 使用共享包：利用 @repo/* 包进行电汇合约和配置
  • 在打开 PR 之前运行 make ci （请参阅贡献）

服务沟通

工作人员通过服务绑定进行通信：

// Call a business Worker over RPC
const result = await env.ACCOUNT_SERVICE.getAccount(userId);

// Call another business Worker
const completion = await env.WORKER_GENAI.completion(request);

AI代理指令

  • AGENTS.md - 跨工具项目约定和 Cursor 的根指令。
  • CLAUDE.md - 克劳德代码入口点；根据 Claude 内存文档导入 AGENTS.md。
  • 每个应用程序/包 - 每个工作区都有匹配的 AGENTS.md 和 CLAUDE.md。
  • hooks/README.md - 共享代理挂钩脚本（不是 Husky）。
  • 规则 - .cursor/rules/**/*.mdc 和 .claude/rules/**/*.md 下的镜像树。
  • 技能 - .agents/skills/ 下的真实来源（请参阅技能 monorepo-agent-setup）。
  • 安全性 - .cursorignore 减少了模型上下文，但不是访问控制边界。

共享包 (@repo/*)

本地包位于packages/下。 每个包都有自己的自述文件。

可用的共享包

  • @repo/dtos-common - Zod 通过子路径发送合约：/api、/rpc、/queue、/webhook
  • @repo/enums-common - 共享约束字符串值（作为 const 对象）
  • @repo/typescript-config - TypeScript 预设（strict.json、workers.json、workers-lib.json、vite-react.json、vite-node.json）

共享包的好处

  • 代码共享：消除工作人员之间的重复
  • 一致性：集中配置和实用程序
  • 轻松更新：更新一次，传播给所有工作人员
  • 类型安全：共享 TypeScript 配置确保一致性

如何使用共享包

  1. 添加到您的worker的package.json中： "dependency": { "@repo/dtos-common": "workspace:*", "@repo/enums-common": "workspace:*", "@repo/typescript-config": "workspace:*" }
  2. 在代码中导入和使用： import { HttpMethod } from "@repo/enums-common";从“@repo/dtos-common/api”导入{ HealthResponseSchema }；
  3. 开发工作流程：共享包中的更改会立即反映在工作人员中 添加新的共享包依赖项后运行 make install

服务绑定

服务绑定允许 Worker 直接相互通信，而无需通过可公开访问的 URL。 它们提供了微服务架构所提供的关注点分离，而没有配置难题、性能开销或学习 RPC 协议的需要。

主要优点

  • 零开销：工作线程在同一线程上运行，提供零延迟
  • 不仅仅是 HTTP：使用 JavaScript 函数在 Worker 之间直接进行方法调用
  • 无额外费用：服务绑定不会增加 Cloudflare 定价
  • 安全通信：无需公共 URL

配置

将服务绑定添加到工作人员的 wrangler.jsonc：

RPC方法调用

RPC 要求被调用者扩展 WorkerEntrypoint 并公开公共方法。 当您传递每个绑定的 Worker 的配置时，调用者会从 wrangler 类型中获取类型化的 env.BINDING.method() 存根（请参阅 Workers RPC - TypeScript）。

被叫者（工人姓名）：

import { WorkerEntrypoint } from "cloudflare:workers";

export default class extends WorkerEntrypoint {
  async fetch() {
    return new Response("ok");
  }
  doSomething(input: string) {
    return { input, ok: true };
  }
}

调用者（例如worker-api）：

export default {
  async fetch(_request: Request, env: Env): Promise<Response> {
    const result = await env.BUSINESS_LOGIC_SERVICE.doSomething("payload");
    return Response.json(result);
  },
} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;

添加绑定后在调用者上重新生成类型：

wrangler types -c ./wrangler.jsonc -c ../worker-name/wrangler.jsonc
